rationale_for_baseline_tier: A patient ~10 days after a checkpoint-inhibitor infusion presents with >10 watery stools/day, inability to tolerate oral fluids, orthostatic near-syncope, profound weakness and tachycardia — early hypovolaemic/distributive shock from a severe immune-related adverse event (immune-mediated colitis). This is an immediate threat to life (circulatory collapse, acute kidney injury, and risk of perforation/toxic megacolon) requiring an emergency ambulance for IV resuscitation and hospital corticosteroid treatment. The patient is bedbound and near-syncopal and cannot safely self-transport, so T5 (call 000) is the only appropriate tier. The cancer-fear framing is not what drives the tier — the shock signs and the immunotherapy timing do.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening dizziness, a faint or collapse, or loss of consciousness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — stay on the line with 000
escalation_edges.rationale: Progression of shock; lay him flat with legs raised, keep him warm, and be ready to start CPR under dispatcher guidance if he stops breathing.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he faints, collapses, or you can't wake him properly, lay him flat and raise his legs, tell 000 straight away, and follow their instructions — start CPR if he stops breathing normally and you're guided to.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blood in the stool, or a hard, severely distended or exquisitely painful abdomen
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests bowel perforation or toxic megacolon — a surgical emergency complicating severe colitis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see blood in the diarrhoea, or his tummy becomes hard, very swollen or severely painful, tell 000 immediately — this is a further emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New breathlessness, chest pain or palpitations
escalation_edges.rationale: Checkpoint-inhibitor toxicity can affect other organs (immune pneumonitis/myocarditis); new cardiorespiratory symptoms are a further emergency and must be reported to paramedics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes short of breath, gets chest pain, or his heart is racing or pounding, tell 000 and the paramedics — his cancer treatment can affect the lungs and heart too.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: On checkpoint-inhibitor immunotherapy for metastatic melanoma
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Immune-related toxicity can be rapidly progressive and multi-organ; maintain a low threshold for emergency escalation and ensure the immunotherapy drug name and last-infusion date are handed to paramedics, as they change hospital treatment (corticosteroid).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is the informant and sole person present with a bedbound, near-syncopal patient
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the spouse clear, simple actions — call 000, keep him flat with legs raised, unlock the door, stay and monitor — and do not rely on the patient to act for himself.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now. Tell them he's had severe watery diarrhoea for two days, can't keep fluids down, and is nearly fainting when he stands, and that he's on cancer immunotherapy. Keep him lying flat with his legs raised and do not let him stand or walk. Have the immunotherapy drug name and last-infusion date ready for the paramedics. Do not give anti-diarrhoea tablets. Unlock the front door and stay with him.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this cannot wait for the morning oncology clinic.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary; the oncology team should document the immune-related adverse event and its grading and review the ongoing immunotherapy plan.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats the presentation as simple gastroenteritis/food poisoning and advises home oral rehydration or a GP visit in the morning.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progressive hypovolaemic shock, acute kidney injury and death from untreated severe immune colitis, or a syncopal fall.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Sends the patient to an urgent same-day GP/urgent care rather than an emergency ambulance despite clear shock signs and inability to self-transport.
